questions.question: Why can't translation be represented as a matrix multiplication in standard 2D Cartesian coordinates? (2-3 marks)
expectedPoints: Translation is an additive operation (x+tx, y+ty).
Matrix multiplication for 2x2 matrices is a linear operation that can only represent combinations of scaling and rotation (multiplicative operations).
No 2x2 matrix M exists such that M * [x, y]^T = [x+tx, y+ty]^T for all x, y.

questions.question: Derive the composite transformation matrix to rotate a 2D object by an angle θ about a fixed point P(h, k). Show each step and explain the sequence. (5-10 marks)
expectedPoints: The pivot point P(h,k) is not at the origin, so rotation cannot be applied directly.
Step 1: Translate the object by T(-h, -k) to move point P to the origin.
Step 2: Apply the standard rotation matrix R(θ) around the origin.
Step 3: Translate the object back by T(h, k) to move the pivot back to its original position.
The composite matrix M is given by the multiplication: M = T(h, k) * R(θ) * T(-h, -k).
Explain that multiplication order is right-to-left: T(-h,-k) is applied first to the point, then R(θ), then T(h,k).
Write out the full matrix multiplication or the final composite matrix elements.
